name: 8355 Method
description: A 3x3 method created by Gilles Roux (the creator of the Roux method) that breaks the solve into four distinct phases based on the number of pieces solved in each step: 8 pieces, then 3 more, then 5 more, then the final 5. It is a corners-first variant that solves all corners first, then tackles edges in groups. The method is notable for its unconventional approach and low algorithm count.

steps.name: Solve 8 Corners
steps.description: Solve all 8 corners of the cube. This can be done using a corners-first approach — first solve the 4 bottom corners, then orient and permute the 4 top corners. With practice, all 8 corners can be solved intuitively or with minimal algorithms.
tips: Learn to solve bottom corners intuitively
Use Sune and anti-Sune for top corner orientation
Practice planning corner solutions during inspection

steps.name: Solve 3 Middle-Layer Edges
steps.description: Insert 3 of the 4 middle-layer edges into their correct positions. Leave one middle-layer slot empty to use as a working space for subsequent steps.
tips: Use the empty slot as a keyhole for easier insertion
Choose which edge to leave out strategically

steps.name: Solve 5 Edges (Top Layer + Last Middle Edge)
steps.description: Solve the 4 top-layer edges and the remaining middle-layer edge simultaneously. This step uses the empty middle slot to manipulate edges into place. Typically involves orienting and then permuting the edges.
tips: Use M and U moves for efficient manipulation
This is the trickiest step — practice edge recognition

steps.name: Solve Last 5 Edges (Bottom Layer + Remaining)
steps.description: Solve the final 4 bottom-layer edges plus any remaining edge to complete the cube. This typically involves flipping the cube and using edge-cycling techniques.
tips: This step becomes very fast with practice

pros: Unique corners-first approach builds understanding of the cube
Created by Gilles Roux, a respected method designer
Good for developing intuitive solving skills
cons: Higher move count than Roux or CFOP
Edge steps can be tricky for beginners
creator: Gilles Roux
